text: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry. [Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html). [Industry](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/industrie/index.html). 16/07/2025 • 4 minutes reading. [David Kemps](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/authors/david-kemps/index.html).

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text: Since the launch of the first Makers Top 100, there has been plenty of discussion about who is in it and why. If you delve deeper into the figures, you will see striking differences between types of ownership. What does it say about a company if it is owned by a family, private equity fund or listed on the stock exchange? And what does that mean for the profitability and resilience of the manufacturing industry? One of the striking findings of the Makers Top 100 published by Link Magazine in February 2025 is that companies with a private equity owner are on average more profitable than family businesses.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text: Dirk Harm Eijssen (Gwynt) and David Kemps (ABN AMRO) delved into the data and saw: the differences are interesting, but not always black and white.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text:
This profitability is measured by the EBITDA percentage: a commonly used financial indicator that shows how much a company makes from operating activities, before deduction of interest, taxes and depreciation, divided by net turnover. [Read more about the Makers Top 100](/nl/business/insights/sectors-and-trends/industry/makers-top-100-good-for-102-billion-turnover-and-300000-jobs.html). Calculated over a three-year period, the average EBITA percentage at the Makers Top 100 companies with a majority or minority stake from a private equity owner is approximately 14 percent compared to 12.5 percent for family businesses. But where does that difference come from? “This is because private equity parties mainly focus on companies that are already profitable and where there is still room for improvement,” says Dirk Harm Eijssen. “They don't just step into something. In almost all industries you see that PE companies perform slightly better on EBITDA percentages than family businesses.”
Steer tighter.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text: According to David Kemps, scale plays an important role.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text:
"Private equity looks for platform companies with growth potential and then expands them through acquisitions. This creates economies of scale and usually leads to more efficient operations and better margins.". Yet it is not just about purchasing power or scale. “Our experience is that family businesses often still have a lot of potential,” Eijssen adds. “We regularly see that when a private equity company takes over a family business, profitability increases by 10 to 20 percent. Not by investing heavily, but by simply managing processes and efficiency more closely.”
Strong rise of private equity. Despite these differences in approach, family businesses still form a significant part of the Dutch manufacturing industry. If large corporates such as ASML and Philips are not taken into account, it is estimated that around 40 percent of manufacturing companies are family or majority-owned companies.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text: A share that is under pressure. For example, we see the rapid rise of private equity in the supply chains of high-tech companies. In the chain around ASML, for example, more than 80 percent of the suppliers are now (partly) owned by PE parties. “In recent years there have been ideal conditions for investors,” says Eijssen. “ASML was growing rapidly and investors wanted to benefit from this. Many suppliers grew explosively, also through internationalization and expansion of production facilities in Europe, North America and Asia.”. At the same time, this growth also places other demands on companies. ASML expects that partners can grow, preferably in size and strength. Kemps: “A supplier worth 30 million euros is less attractive for a party like ASML than one worth 300 million.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text: In this market you have to be able to withstand a blow if things go wrong for two or three years.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text:
From that perspective, scaling up is almost necessary.”
Entrepreneurship cannot be consolidated. Yet the private equity model also has limits. “Most investors want to make their exit within five to seven years,” says Eijssen. “That is a relatively short term and they therefore often avoid (too) much risk. That is why in markets where the business case is not yet crystal clear, you see that family businesses are more inclined to invest.”. According to Eijssen, entrepreneurship is essential for the future. "Of course efficiency is important. But to really innovate and enter new markets, you need courage and intuition. Family (and director-majority) businesses have traditionally been strong in this. They dare to invest based on intuition and drive, even without firm commitments from customers."
Short term versus long term. According to both speakers, the rise of private equity and consolidation in the sector are understandable and necessary. “Scale is needed, especially with the current labor shortage and globalization,” says Kemps. "But that focus on the short term and efficiency is not enough. Enterprising family businesses remain indispensable for the long term.". Eijssen does not see it as a contradiction, but as a balance that must be sought. “Private equity companies have an important role in professionalizing companies and creating strength. But you also need companies that dare and innovate, even when things get exciting or the business case is not obvious.”. According to Kemps and Eijssen, both groups can learn from each other. “Family businesses can benefit from the tighter processes and financial discipline that investors bring,” says Eijssen. “And private equity companies can draw inspiration from the long-term vision and entrepreneurship of family businesses.”.

metadata.source_title: Family businesses and private equity are putting their own stamp on the industry

text:
